expanded_description: Understanding the Brønsted-Lowry theory of acids and bases, which defines acids as proton donors and bases as proton acceptors, extending acid-base concepts beyond aqueous solutions.

core_concept.principle: Brønsted-Lowry theory defines acids as proton ($\text{H}^+$) donors and bases as proton ($\text{H}^+$) acceptors, focusing on proton transfer rather than ion production.
core_concept.explanation: Proposed independently by Johannes Brønsted and Thomas Lowry in 1923, this theory expanded the Arrhenius definition by removing the requirement for aqueous solution and hydroxide ions. Any reaction involving proton transfer is an acid-base reaction. This explains the basicity of substances like ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) that contain no $\text{OH}^-$ but accept protons from water.

image.alt: Diagram showing proton transfer from acid to base
image.caption: Brønsted-Lowry acids donate $\text{H}^+$; bases accept $\text{H}^+$

sections.introduction: While Arrhenius theory explained acids and bases in water, it failed to account for acid-base behavior in other solvents or gas-phase reactions. The Brønsted-Lowry theory, developed in 1923, generalized the concept by focusing on the transfer of protons ($\text{H}^+$ ions) rather than the production of specific ions in water.

key_concept.definition: A Brønsted-Lowry acid is any substance that donates a proton ($\text{H}^+$); a Brønsted-Lowry base is any substance that accepts a proton ($\text{H}^+$).
key_concept.context: This definition applies to aqueous solutions, non-aqueous solvents, and gas-phase reactions, making it broader than Arrhenius theory.

principles.statement: Acid-base reactions involve the transfer of a proton from an acid to a base.
principles.application: Identify the acid as the species losing $\text{H}^+$ and the base as the species gaining $\text{H}^+$ in the reaction equation.
principles.analogy: Like a game of catch—the acid throws the proton (ball); the base catches it.

principles.statement: To act as a Brønsted-Lowry acid, a substance must contain a hydrogen atom that can be released as $\text{H}^+$.
principles.application: Not all H-containing compounds are acids (e.g., $\text{CH}_4$); the H must be ionizable/transferable.
principles.example: $\text{CH}_4$ contains H but does not donate $\text{H}^+$; $\text{HCl}$ contains H and readily donates $\text{H}^+$.
principles.analogy: Like having money in a locked vault vs. cash in hand—both have value, but only cash can be donated immediately.

components.definition: The Brønsted-Lowry model focuses on the reactants and products involved in proton transfer.
components.equation: $\text{HA} + \text{B} \rightleftharpoons \text{A}^- + \text{HB}^+$ where HA = acid, B = base, $\text{A}^-$ = conjugate base, $\text{HB}^+$ = conjugate acid
components.interpretation: The acid (HA) becomes its conjugate base ($\text{A}^-$) after losing $\text{H}^+$; the base (B) becomes its conjugate acid ($\text{HB}^+$) after gaining $\text{H}^+$.

image.alt: Diagram showing HA transferring H+ to B
image.caption: Proton transfer converts acid to conjugate base and base to conjugate acid

worked_examples.title: Identifying Brønsted-Lowry Acids and Bases
worked_examples.problem: In the reaction $\text{HF}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+(aq) + \text{F}^-(aq)$, identify the Brønsted-Lowry acid and base.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify which species loses $\text{H}^+$ and which gains $\text{H}^+$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Brønsted-Lowry acid = proton donor; base = proton acceptor.

worked_examples.title: Gas-Phase Acid-Base Reaction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the acid and base in the gas-phase reaction: $\text{NH}_3(g) + \text{HCl}(g) \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}(s)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Arrhenius theory cannot explain this (no water); Brønsted-Lowry can

worked_examples.final_answer: Acid: $\text{HCl}$; Base: $\text{NH}_3$ (Brønsted-Lowry theory explains non-aqueous reactions)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Brønsted-Lowry theory applies to gas-phase and non-aqueous systems.

sections.key_concept: When a Brønsted-Lowry acid donates a proton, it becomes a conjugate base. When a base accepts a proton, it becomes a conjugate acid. These related species form conjugate pairs.

classifications.value: The species formed when an acid loses a proton
characteristics: Has one less $\text{H}^+$ than the acid
Has one more negative charge (or one less positive)
Can act as a base in reverse reaction
classifications.behavior: Strength inversely related to parent acid strength (strong acid → weak conjugate base)

classifications.value: The species formed when a base gains a proton
characteristics: Has one more $\text{H}^+$ than the base
Has one more positive charge (or one less negative)
Can act as an acid in reverse reaction
classifications.behavior: Strength inversely related to parent base strength (strong base → weak conjugate acid)

classifications.value: Acid and conjugate base (or base and conjugate acid) differ by exactly one proton
characteristics: Differ by one $\text{H}^+$
Differ by one unit of charge
Linked by proton transfer
classifications.behavior: Identify pairs by finding species that differ by exactly one $\text{H}^+$

worked_examples.title: Identifying Conjugate Acid-Base Pairs
worked_examples.problem: Identify the two conjugate acid-base pairs in the reaction: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+(aq) + \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-(aq)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Look for pairs on opposite sides of the equation

worked_examples.concept_applied: Conjugate pairs differ by exactly one proton ($\text{H}^+$).

worked_examples.title: Finding Conjugate Acids and Bases
worked_examples.problem: What is the conjugate base of $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$? What is the conjugate acid of $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$?
worked_examples.final_answer: Conjugate base of $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$: $\text{HPO}_4^{2-}$; Conjugate acid of $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$: $\text{HSO}_4^-$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Conjugate base = remove $\text{H}^+$; Conjugate acid = add $\text{H}^+$.

sections.introduction: Some substances can act as either acids or bases depending on the reaction conditions. These versatile substances play crucial roles in buffering systems and chemical equilibria.

key_concept.definition: Amphiprotic substances can either donate or accept a proton; amphoteric substances can act as either an acid or a base (broader term including non-proton systems).
key_concept.context: In Brønsted-Lowry theory, water is the most common example of an amphiprotic substance.

categories.description: Water can act as either an acid or a base depending on the reactant
categories.detailed_mechanism: With strong acid, water accepts $\text{H}^+$ (base); with strong base, water donates $\text{H}^+$ (acid)

categories.description: Certain ions can donate or accept protons depending on conditions
categories.detailed_mechanism: Often intermediate ions from polyprotic acid ionization

worked_examples.title: Demonstrating Amphiprotic Behavior
worked_examples.problem: Write two equations showing how $\text{HCO}_3^-$ can act as both a Brønsted-Lowry acid and a Brønsted-Lowry base.

step_by_step.explanation: Check that $\text{H}^+$ is donated in first equation and accepted in second
step_by_step.calculation: Both equations show valid proton transfer

worked_examples.concept_applied: Amphiprotic substances can donate or accept protons depending on the reaction partner.

sections.key_concept: Brønsted-Lowry theory resolved several limitations of Arrhenius theory, providing a more comprehensive framework for understanding acid-base chemistry.

classifications.value: Explains acid-base behavior in solvents other than water
characteristics: Applies to liquid $\text{NH}_3$, acetic acid, etc.
No water required for proton transfer
Gas-phase reactions included
classifications.behavior: Proton transfer can occur in any medium that allows ion mobility or direct contact

examples.explanation: Forms $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ without water; Arrhenius cannot explain

classifications.value: Explains basicity of substances without $\text{OH}^-$ groups
characteristics: Includes $\text{NH}_3$, amines, carbonates
Focus on proton acceptance, not $\text{OH}^-$ production
Unified explanation for all bases
classifications.behavior: Any species with lone pair electrons can potentially accept $\text{H}^+$

examples.explanation: Accepts $\text{H}^+$ to form $\text{NH}_4^+$; no $\text{OH}^-$ in formula

classifications.value: Introduces relationship between acids and bases through conjugate pairs
characteristics: Every acid has a conjugate base
Every base has a conjugate acid
Explains reversible reactions
classifications.behavior: Acid-base reactions viewed as competition for protons between conjugate pairs

examples.explanation: Reaction favors formation of weaker acid-base pair

worked_examples.title: Explaining Ammonia Basicity
worked_examples.problem: Explain why ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) is a base using Brønsted-Lowry theory, even though it contains no $\text{OH}^-$.

step_by_step.calculation: Arrhenius explanation requires indirect reaction with water

step_by_step.explanation: Brønsted-Lowry directly explains basicity without requiring $\text{OH}^-$ in formula

worked_examples.final_answer: Ammonia is a Brønsted-Lowry base because it accepts protons ($\text{H}^+$) to form $\text{NH}_4^+$, regardless of $\text{OH}^-$ presence
worked_examples.concept_applied: Brønsted-Lowry theory explains basicity of non-hydroxide substances.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about proton transfer and conjugate pairs can lead to incorrect identifications. Clear strategies ensure accurate analysis.

classifications.value: Forgetting to adjust charge when adding or removing $\text{H}^+$
characteristics: Conjugate base has one more negative charge
Conjugate acid has one more positive charge
Charge conservation violated
classifications.behavior: Always check charge balance when identifying conjugates

classifications.value: Pairing species that don't differ by exactly one proton
characteristics: Matching acid with unrelated base
Ignoring stoichiometry
Missing conjugate relationship
classifications.behavior: Conjugate pairs must appear on opposite sides of equation and differ by one $\text{H}^+$

examples.explanation: Wrong pair: $\text{HCl}/\text{NaOH}$; Correct pairs: $\text{HCl}/\text{Cl}^-$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}/\text{OH}^-$ (in net ionic)

classifications.type: Error: Assuming All H Are Acidic
classifications.value: Treating any H-containing compound as a Brønsted-Lowry acid
characteristics: Ignoring bond strength
Overlooking non-ionizable H
Classifying hydrocarbons as acids
classifications.behavior: Only ionizable/transferable H defines Brønsted-Lowry acidity

examples.explanation: Contains H but C-H bonds too strong to donate $\text{H}^+$ in normal conditions; not an acid

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Charge in Conjugate Pairs
worked_examples.problem: A student claims the conjugate base of $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ is $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: Conjugate base differs by exactly one $\text{H}^+$
Conjugate pairs differ by exactly one proton and one unit of charge

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: removed 3 protons; correct conjugate base is $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$ (differs by one $\text{H}^+$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Conjugate acid-base pairs differ by exactly one proton and one charge unit.

worked_examples.title: Brønsted-Lowry Analysis Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic approach for analyzing Brønsted-Lowry acid-base reactions.

step_by_step.explanation: Ensure atoms and charge are balanced
Identify which species loses $\text{H}^+$ and which gains $\text{H}^+$
step_by_step.calculation: Draw arrow from acid H to base

step_by_step.explanation: Find species differing by one $\text{H}^+$ on opposite sides
step_by_step.calculation: Check charge difference matches H difference

step_by_step.explanation: Check if any species can act as both acid and base

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow 5-step checklist: balance equation, track protons, identify acid/base, identify conjugates, verify amphiprotic
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic approach ensures accurate Brønsted-Lowry analysis.

summary: Brønsted-Lowry theory (1923) defines acids as proton ($\text{H}^+$) donors and bases as proton ($\text{H}^+$) acceptors, focusing on proton transfer rather than ion production in water. This theory extends Arrhenius concepts to non-aqueous solvents and gas-phase reactions, explaining the basicity of substances like $\text{NH}_3$ that contain no $\text{OH}^-$. Key concepts include: (1) Conjugate acid-base pairs—species differing by exactly one $\text{H}^+$ (acid loses $\text{H}^+$ to become conjugate base; base gains $\text{H}^+$ to become conjugate acid); (2) Amphiprotic substances—can act as either acid or base (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{HCO}_3^-$); (3) Proton transfer mechanism—acid-base reactions involve $\text{H}^+$ moving from donor to acceptor. Advantages over Arrhenius theory include broader scope (any solvent, gas phase), explanation of non-hydroxide bases, and introduction of conjugate pair relationships. Common errors include: forgetting charge changes when identifying conjugates, pairing species that don't differ by one proton, and assuming all H-containing compounds are acids. A systematic 5-step analysis (balance equation, track protons, identify acid/base, identify conjugates, verify amphiprotic) ensures accurate application. Mastery of Brønsted-Lowry theory provides the foundation for understanding acid-base equilibria, pH calculations, buffer systems, and titration behavior in subsequent sections.
